coredata.dc:title: Edge-functionalized graphene quantum dot as a nano-biosensor for simultaneous detection of glucose, galactose and lactose
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yoosefian, Mehdi
Ebrahimpour, Yeganeh
coredata.dc:description: The swift and accurate diagnosis of diseases, particularly diabetes, remains a pressing and critical concern in healthcare. Monitoring blood glucose levels is imperative for diabetic patients to manage their condition effectively. Addressing this necessity, developing non-invasive, simultaneous detection devices for sugars shows immense promise. Herein, we introduce the design of a novel nano-biosensor employing edge‐functionalized graphene quantum dots, enabling the concurrent detection of three pivotal sugars: glucose, galactose, and lactose. Through comprehensive molecular dynamics simulations, we demonstrate the feasibility and stability of the proposed biosensor. Our results show that the edge-functionalization of graphene quantum dots significantly enhances their binding affinity, with calculated average number of contacts of 2174 for glucose, 1085 for galactose, and 6274 for lactose. Furthermore, the radial distribution function analysis echoed these findings, highlighting lactose’s pronounced interaction with the biosensor, followed by glucose and then galactose. The detailed analysis reveals distinct and non-overlapping sugar bindings on the nano-biosensor surface, with lactose showing the highest affinity and interaction strength, followed by glucose and galactose. This innovative approach marks a significant stride forward in biosensor technology, providing a potential avenue for precise and dependable monitoring of blood sugar levels in diabetic patients.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Edge‐functionalized graphene quantum dots
Nano-biosensor
Molecular dynamics simulation
Glucose
Galactose
Lactose

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical structure of investigated systems; lactose (green), glucose (yellow), galactose (red) and edge functionalized graphene quantum dot (blue). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Snapshots taken during the 100 ns simulation of the biosensor system alongside glucose, galactose, and lactose sugars at (A) t = 50 ns and the (B) final state at t = 100 ns. lactose (green), glucose (yellow), galactose (red) and edge functionalized graphene quantum dot (blue). For clarity, water and virtual sites are not shown. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The root mean square deviation diagram that shows the dynamic behavior of each component of the investigated system.
(A) Minimum distance and (B) contact number analysis depicting the proximity and interactions between the biosensor and sugars.
The number of hydrogen bonds (A) between the water molecules and galactose, glucose and lactose; (B) between the biosensor and galactose, glucose and lactose.

(A) Radial distribution function illustrating the intermolecular interactions between the biosensor and three distinct sugars; (B) density profiles of galactose sugar (blue), glucose sugar (red), and lactose sugar (green). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Density of states for galactose sugar (blue), glucose sugar (red), and lactose sugar (green). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Edge-functionalized graphene quantum dot as a nano-biosensor for simultaneous detection of glucose, galactose and lactose

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Graduate University of Advanced Technology, Kerman, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Graduate University of Advanced Technology, Kerman, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanotechnology, Graduate University of Advanced Technology, Kerman, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Nanotechnology, Graduate University of Advanced Technology, Kerman, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Graduate University of Advanced Technology, Kerman, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The swift and accurate diagnosis of diseases, particularly diabetes, remains a pressing and critical concern in healthcare. Monitoring blood glucose levels is imperative for diabetic patients to manage their condition effectively. Addressing this necessity, developing non-invasive, simultaneous detection devices for sugars shows immense promise. Herein, we introduce the design of a novel nano-biosensor employing edge‐functionalized graphene quantum dots, enabling the concurrent detection of three pivotal sugars: glucose, galactose, and lactose. Through comprehensive molecular dynamics simulations, we demonstrate the feasibility and stability of the proposed biosensor. Our results show that the edge-functionalization of graphene quantum dots significantly enhances their binding affinity, with calculated average number of contacts of 2174 for glucose, 1085 for galactose, and 6274 for lactose. Furthermore, the radial distribution function analysis echoed these findings, highlighting lactose’s pronounced interaction with the biosensor, followed by glucose and then galactose. The detailed analysis reveals distinct and non-overlapping sugar bindings on the nano-biosensor surface, with lactose showing the highest affinity and interaction strength, followed by glucose and galactose. This innovative approach marks a significant stride forward in biosensor technology, providing a potential avenue for precise and dependable monitoring of blood sugar levels in diabetic patients.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetes stands as a formidable global health challenge, contributing significantly to mortality and disability worldwide [1]. Recent surveys conducted in 2014 revealed that over 387 million individuals worldwide are afflicted with this condition [2], with projections by the International Diabetes Association estimating this number to soar to 592 million by 2035. In light of this escalating prevalence, the accurate determination of glucose concentration, particularly in blood, emerges as a critical imperative. Diabetes, characterized by abnormal blood glucose levels, stems from impaired insulin production or reduced responsiveness to insulin, hampering its regulatory function [3,4]. With two primary classifications, type 1 diabetes manifests from beta cell destruction, while type 2 diabetes entails progressive insulin resistance, often exacerbated by genetic predisposition, obesity, and sedentary lifestyle [5–7].

Consequently, impaired glucose utilization precipitates hyperglycemia [8], fostering microvascular damage and predisposing individuals to renal, ocular, and neurological complications [9]. Notably, diabetes confers heightened susceptibility to cardiovascular diseases, underscoring the imperative for early detection and intervention [10]. Responding to burgeoning healthcare demands, diverse glucose measuring biosensors have been developed, leveraging advancements in medical science and diagnostic methodologies [11–13]. Efforts are ongoing to enhance the accuracy and user-friendliness of these biosensors, reflecting a commitment to refining glucose monitoring techniques [14]. Biosensors constitute a class of analytical instruments featuring a biological sensory receptor with diverse applications spanning medical diagnostics, food safety, environmental monitoring, and defense [15]. The genesis of biosensor technology traces back to 1962 with the seminal development of enzyme electrodes by Leland C. Clark, marking the inception of biosensor research [16]. Essentially, biosensors comprise a fixed biological component that undergoes discernible alterations in response to the target analyte, thereby modulating the surrounding environment [17]. The ensuing transduction of these changes into electrical signals, decipherable by downstream electronic converters, underscores the interdisciplinary nature of biosensor technology, integrating principles from engineering, chemistry, physics, biochemistry, and biology [18]. Within this expansive landscape, nano biosensors, leveraging nanomaterials to convert biological responses into electrical signals, represent a notable advancement [19,20]. The utilization of nanomaterials affords nano biosensors a remarkable surface-to-volume ratio, enhancing sensitivity and precision in analyte detection [20–24]. Central to the functionality of biosensors are the biological recognition elements, which selectively bind to specific substrates without cross-reactivity. Enzymes, antibodies, nucleic acids, and receptors emerge as the primary categories of these recognition elements [25–27].

Enzymes, as proteins, function as pivotal biological catalysts that expedite chemical reactions within biological systems [28]. They enhance reaction rates by catalyzing the conversion of substrates–molecules targeted by enzymes–into distinct molecular products. Given their essential role in catalyzing virtually all metabolic processes within the cell, enzyme catalysis is fundamental for maintaining cellular metabolism and sustaining life [28].

This study focuses on three key enzymes:
(a)
Glucose oxidase (GOx): Discovered by Müller in 1928, GOx has since been extensively employed in biosensor research [29]. Structurally, GOx comprises two similar polypeptide chains, each with a molecular weight ranging from 150 to 186 kDa. Flavin adenine dinucleotide (FAD) and iron molecules are bound to each chain, linked by disulfide bonds. GOx, a flavoprotein, exhibits an isoelectric point of 4.5, with a negative charge at pHs above its isoelectric point and a positive charge at lower pHs.
(b)
Galactokinase: Galactokinase, a member of the phosphotransferase enzyme group, orchestrates the phosphorylation of alpha-D-galactose, leading to the formation of galactose 1-phosphate [30]. This enzymatic process, necessitating the hydrolysis of one ATP molecule, constitutes the second pivotal step in the Leloir pathway, a conserved metabolic pathway prevalent across diverse living organisms, wherein beta-D-galactose is metabolically converted into phosphate. Initially isolated from mammalian liver tissue, galactokinase plays a central role in galactose metabolism.
(c)
Lactase: A member of the beta-galactosidase enzyme family, lactase is predominantly found in the differentiated cells lining the villi of the small intestine [31]. Essential for lactose hydrolysis, lactase deficiency manifests as lactose intolerance, characterized by digestive discomfort following milk consumption. In metabolism, lactase catalyzes the hydrolysis of the beta-glycosidic bond in D-lactose, yielding D-galactose and D-glucose, facilitating their absorption through intestinal epithelial cells and into the bloodstream.

In this study, these enzymes are strategically harnessed to fabricate a novel nano-biosensor platform. By situating the active sites of these enzymes at the edge of graphene quantum dots, we aim to fabricate a nano-biosensor capable of simultaneously detecting three sugars: glucose, galactose, and lactose.

Accurate and simultaneous detection of multiple sugars remains a significant challenge in biosensor technology. This study introduces a novel nano-biosensor platform that addresses this limitation by integrating edge-functionalized graphene quantum dots with specific enzyme active sites. This innovative approach synergistically leverages the exceptional properties of both components, resulting in enhanced sensitivity, specificity, and efficiency in glucose monitoring. To our knowledge, this is the first demonstration of a biosensor capable of concurrently detecting glucose, galactose, and lactose, offering a promising avenue for comprehensive metabolic profiling. The potential clinical implications of this research are substantial. By enabling precise and rapid multi-sugar detection, the developed biosensor can significantly contribute to improved diabetes management, early diagnosis of metabolic disorders, and personalized treatment strategies. The integration of this technology into point-of-care devices could revolutionize patient care by facilitating self-monitoring and empowering individuals to actively participate in their healthcare.

Computer simulations serve as a fundamental approach for deriving the properties of a molecular ensemble, leveraging the structural configuration and intricate microscopic interactions among molecules. These simulations function as a conduit, facilitating the integration of observations from the microscopic domain, characterized by distinct length and time scales, into the macroscopic milieu of laboratory experimentation. Molecular dynamics simulation represents a computationally reliable methodology where in the examination of microscopic properties enables the derivation of macroscopic properties within the system of interest. This method entails the interaction of atoms within the system under specified conditions and over a defined temporal duration. A force field, comprising classical potential energy terms and adjustable parameters, governs these interactions. The selection of an appropriate force field is contingent upon the chemical composition of the system and the nature of the molecules under investigation. Commonly employed force fields for organic and biological systems include Gromos, Charmm, Amber, and optimization potential for liquid simulation (Opls-aa). In this study, the Charmm36 force field is employed to model all parameters governing bonding and non-bonding interactions.

To prepare the nano-biosensor and carry out the simulation, the PDB files related to the three enzymes Galactokinase (code: 2AJ4), glucose oxidase (code: 1GPE), and lactase (code: 6TSK) were obtained from the RCSB site [32–34]. Then, the active sites of these three enzymes have been determined as follows: Galactokinase (Arg36, Glu42, Asp45, Asp183, Tyr233), Glucose Oxidase (His559, His516, Asn514, Arg512, Thr110, Fad600), and Lactase (His418, Phe416, Asp199, His391, Asp201, Tyr100, Trp5304, Ph5305, His, Tyr503). Subsequently, these residues were added to the edge of the graphene quantum dots to generate a novel nano-biosensor.

Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations were conducted using Gromacs software version 5.1.4, and all processes were observed throughout the simulation using VMD software [35,36]. A simulation box with dimensions of 7 nm × 7 nm × 7 nm was constructed to investigate the interactions between the nano-biosensor and sugars. Subsequently, sugars were introduced into the simulation box, and the spce water model from the chosen force field was employed. No ions were added to the simulation box due to the neutrality of the designed system. Molecular dynamics simulations were performed under both NPT (isothermal-isobaric) and NVT (Canonical Ensemble) conditions. The temperature was held constant at 300 K using the V-rescale thermostat while maintaining a pressure of 1 bar with the Berendsen algorithm and a time step of 0.1 ps. Periodic boundary conditions (PBC) were applied to maintain system integrity and reduce surface effects, ensuring that the simulation mimicked an infinite, continuous environment. An accelerator algorithm has been employed to optimize efficiency while minimizing energy consumption. Van der Waals interactions were modeled and scrutinized utilizing a specified cutoff distance. The equations of motion were integrated using the PEM algorithm for electrostatic interactions with a time step of 2 fs and a total time of 100 ns. The chemical structure of biosensor system, as well as glucose, galactose, and lactose sugars, are presented in Fig. 1 .

In this section, we present a comprehensive analysis of the dynamic simulation trajectories to scrutinize the intricate interactions between the biosensor and sugars. Root-mean-square deviation (RMSD) analysis of the system, characterization of hydrogen bonding patterns between the biosensor and sugars, determination of intermolecular distances, quantification of the number of contacts, evaluation of radial distribution functions for the biosensor and individual sugars, assessment of total system energy, and examination of density about each sugar are meticulously scrutinized. Visual representations of the configurations of the biosensor system, as well as glucose, galactose, and lactose sugars, are presented in Fig. 2 .

Stability assessment of the system involves the analysis of total energy, a crucial parameter. The endeavor to minimize energy aims at attaining the system's most stable state. Energy minimization mitigates spatial congestion and improper overlap within the system.

Furthermore, the root-mean-square deviation (RMSD) is instrumental in tracking time-dependent structural fluctuations, particularly in assessing the system's stability over time. This analysis also serves to quantify deviations from the initial coordinates throughout the simulation, reflecting any perturbations in the system's equilibrium. In this study, RMSD analysis spanning 100 ns was conducted to scrutinize the detailed structural dynamics and evaluate the stability of the biosensor system. Fig. 3 illustrates the analysis results, demonstrating that the system reached equilibrium over time. The consistent RMSD value indicates the stability and equilibrium of the system throughout the simulation trajectory. Overall, the RMSD analysis indicates the attainment of a relatively stable configuration, as inferred from the average deviation data.

Distance analysis was employed to ascertain the minimum separation between distinct molecular groups, revealing the extent of proximity and adsorption of sugars onto the biosensor surface. This analysis holds paramount importance in substantiating the binding affinity of sugars to the biosensor. As depicted in Fig. 4 A, the dynamic assessment of the minimum distance between the biosensor and glucose, galactose, and lactose sugars was conducted throughout the simulation. Remarkably, the minimum distance between the biosensor and galactose sugar exhibited notable fluctuations, characterized by prolonged peaks indicative of weak adsorption onto the biosensor surface.

Conversely, the outcomes for glucose and lactose sugars manifested enhanced stability, as evidenced by minimal fluctuations and sustained graph integrity. Specifically, the average distance for lactose sugar remained constant at 0.18 nm throughout the simulation, while for glucose sugar, the average distance stood at 0.19 nm, exhibiting marginal variability.

Conversely, the average distance for galactose sugar was notably higher at 0.23 nm, indicative of weaker interaction with the biosensor surface, marked by pronounced graph fluctuations. Overall, the findings of this analysis suggest that lactose sugar, followed by glucose sugar, exhibited superior interaction with the biosensor, underscoring their potential for robust biosensor binding. In contrast, galactose sugar demonstrated weaker adhesive properties, as evidenced by fluctuating graph patterns.

Additionally, to provide deeper insights into the biosensor-sugar interaction, the number of contacts between the biosensor and sugars was computed. As depicted in Fig. 4B, a close correlation and extensive interaction between these entities was observed throughout the simulation period. Specifically, the average number of contacts between the biosensor and lactose sugar was determined to be 6274, whereas for glucose sugar, it averaged 2174. Notably, as depicted in Fig. 4B for galactose sugar, there was an initial decline in the number of contacts at the onset of the simulation, with an average of 1085 contacts between the biosensor and galactose sugar observed. The findings of this section indicate a significantly higher number of contacts between the biosensor and lactose sugar compared to other sugars throughout the simulation. This observation is further supported by the distance analysis depicted in Fig. 4B, validating the robustness of this conclusion.

To further investigate the molecular interactions between the sugars and the biosensor, we employed hydrogen bond analysis. Intramolecular hydrogen bonds denote interactions between proton donor and acceptor groups within the molecules themselves [37–39]. The stability and quantity of these hydrogen bonds formed between the biosensor and the target sugars play a pivotal role in anchoring the sugars onto the biosensor surface. Fig. 5 A illustrates the hydrogen bond counts between the water molecules and sugars (glucose, galactose, and lactose) and Fig. 5B shows these interactions between biosensor and sugars throughout the simulation. Notably, lactose sugar exhibits the highest number of hydrogen bonds with 5 bonds, followed by glucose sugar with 4 bonds, while galactose sugar shows 2 hydrogen bonds. Consequently, lactose sugar demonstrates a superior propensity to form hydrogen bonds compared to other sugars.

Fig. 6 A illustrates the radial distribution function (RDF) depicting the interplay between the biosensor and three distinct sugars (glucose, galactose, and lactose) throughout the simulation. This analysis meticulously evaluates the spatial distribution and density of sugars surrounding the biosensor. Examination of the RDF profile reveals a pronounced peak at approximately 0.6 nm, indicative of a robust interaction between the biosensor and sugars. Further scrutiny unveils a remarkably stronger and more cohesive interaction, specifically between the biosensor and lactose sugar, denoting a compelling affinity between the two entities. Notably, the height of the RDF peak corresponding to lactose sugar surpasses that of glucose and galactose sugars, underscoring its heightened interaction intensity. Conversely, interactions with other sugars appear comparatively weaker. Furthermore, the density profiles of each sugar, delineated by specific coordinates, provide additional insights into their mass-to-volume ratios and spatial arrangements.

In Fig. 6B, the density profiles of three distinct sugars are depicted. The green curve corresponds to lactose sugar within the system, exhibiting the highest density at coordinates of 1 nm. The pronounced and broad peak observed in this graph underscores the elevated density of lactose sugar within the system. In contrast, glucose and galactose sugars exhibit comparatively lower density values at the 1 nm coordinates when compared to lactose sugar.

Fig. 7 illustrates the state density profiles for three sugars: glucose, galactose, and lactose. As depicted in Fig. 7, lactose sugar exhibits the highest state density in the system, averaging 36,435, Glucose sugar follows with a state density of approximately 19,371. In contrast, galactose sugar presents the lowest state density at about 17,093, indicating a comparatively limited availability of states for occupation within this sugar molecule.

This study focused on the design of a functionalized graphene quantum dot biosensor and the identification of active sites within three enzymes: glucose oxidase, galactokinase, and lactase (galactosidase). A simulation box with dimensions of 7 nm × 7 nm × 7 nm was constructed to accommodate the biosensor system along with glucose, galactose, and lactose sugars. Employing molecular dynamics simulation under NPT and NVT conditions for 100 ns, the behavior of this system was thoroughly investigated. The analysis of total energy and root-mean-square deviation throughout the simulation affirmed the stability of the system, demonstrating its robustness. Hydrogen bond analysis revealed the predominant presence of hydrogen bonds with lactose sugar, indicative of strong interactions between lactose and the biosensor.

Additionally, examination of the minimum distance between the biosensor and each sugar underscored the superior absorption of lactose compared to other sugars. Analysis of the number of contacts between the biosensor and sugars further supported the superior affinity of lactose, followed by glucose, with galactose exhibiting the weakest interaction. The radial distribution function analysis echoed these findings, highlighting lactose's pronounced interaction with the biosensor, followed by glucose and then galactose. Moreover, density analysis indicating a superior connection and binding of lactose compared to glucose, and glucose compared to galactose during the simulation.

While our study highlights the potential application of this biosensor technology in diabetes management through the precise detection of multiple sugars, several practical challenges need to be addressed to ensure its successful implementation in real-world scenarios. These challenges include the complexity of sample processing, real-time monitoring, and large-scale production. To tackle the issue of complex sample matrices, advanced microfluidic technologies can be integrated with the biosensor system. Microfluidics can streamline sample preparation, reducing the need for extensive preprocessing steps by automating the separation and purification processes. Additionally, employing robust surface functionalization techniques can enhance the specificity and selectivity of the biosensor, thereby minimizing interference from non-target molecules present in complex biological samples.

This study employed molecular dynamics (MD) simulations to investigate the interactions between a novel graphene quantum dot-based biosensor and three key sugars: glucose, galactose, and lactose. By analyzing key parameters such as hydrogen bonding, intermolecular distances, and contact number, we demonstrated a clear preference for the biosensor for lactose over glucose and galactose. These findings were further corroborated by radial distribution function and density analyses, which highlighted the stronger binding affinity of lactose to the biosensor surface. The results of this study underscore the potential of the proposed biosensor for accurate and simultaneous detection of multiple sugars. The combination of graphene quantum dots and enzyme active sites has shown promise in enhancing sensitivity and specificity, which are critical for effective glucose monitoring and management. Further experimental validation and optimization are necessary to confirm these computational findings into practical biosensor applications. However, the present study provides a strong foundation for the development of advanced diagnostic tools for a variety of metabolic disorders.
Mehdi Yoosefian: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Yeganeh Ebrahimpour: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
